name: Study 2: A Full Game of Bad Habits

comments.text:
This game was so special I decided to include the game in its entirety. One of my subscribers sent it and it features bad habits from both sides.

This was played by players rated around 800 on chess.com.
https://www.chess.com/analysis/game/live/62758762861?tab=analysis
But you've got to know how to deal with this.
Black gave a check.
White unpinned.
Black now captured.
Black traded...
One more move.
And White is up a rook!
But this wasn't the correct continuation!
White started with 1. e4.

Check! 

White wins.

This is a fork.
White played d4, defending e5.
White just takes...
And White is down a piece.
This check can be blocked...
The queen recaptured.
Black started moving to the kingside...
White started bringing another pawn up.
This is exactly the type of counterplay I was saying. It's easily countered, by unpinning, but still. As long as it's avoided...
Black recaptured...
Black recaptured...

Black moved the pawn forward. 

There really isn't much to do at this point. 

The a7 pawn was hanging, though.

Black could've maybe tried saving that.
And after this final push...
Black played d3...

White attacked the rook. 

This is a one move threat that accomplishes nothing!
White just recaptured.
Once it moves away...

White developed. The problem with this move is that it blocks the c-pawn. 

In the Caro-Kann, Black usually plays c5 for counterplay. 

White's traditional response is usually c3, defending d4.
Check winning the pawn!
The king recaptured.

And now Black went e5, without caring about the fact that the queen is on the e-file opposite the king (this is a blunder).

Now, Black is losing a pawn. However, I think it is because I'm a titled player, but even a strong beginner can begin developing these instincts, that I kind of see that if dxe5, fxe5, the queen would "see" the knight on h4. 

I subconsciously see that the h4 knight is undefended and that the queen diagonally could threaten it! This will be relevant in a few moves...
The pawns marching up.

White developed the bishop, threatening the queen.

Any sound response for Black is now better.

And now White is winning! 

Both players give up pieces back and forth because they don't see each other's threats!
If you're a more defensive player, f3 defends the rook's entry point.
c5, but this is over...
If you want to be really surgical, you can take this pawn first...

Nxc5 was an option also, picking up the knight.

To White's credit, they played it!
Black gets the king closer to the pawns to try to stop them.
The knight has to move.
Check!

The knight moved out of b7, but what about Rh8+? 

Rb8 wasn't even threatening to take the knight, so White's fear was irrational.
The knight went to the center...
But now White gets a queen.

Checkmate! 

What a game! 

Both players giving up material back and forth, not aware of each other's threats!
White just took.

And now White completely gave the positional advantage away by giving up a piece.

Bh4 places itself on the knight's view.
And if Rde3...
Black castled...

Black moved their knight away. 

Nc5 defends b7, yet still if b4 the defender is removed...

Another thing to consider is f2 is hanging.
At any rate, Black continued with Bg4, developing the bishop.
And finally this game is over...
White advanced the pawn, but this isn't how you usually play...
Black kicked the knight out and got the pawn out of danger.
The king got out of the pin.
These types of x-rays should be second nature to you.
Single move threats: White attacked the a-pawn.
But Black just developed, overlooking the opponent's plan and threat.
White captured on e5, winning the pawn...

This capture wins a piece.

It removes the defender of the bishop on h4.
The king went to attack the rook, disregarding the h7 pawn...
Black can just defend normally.
White traded...
So far, sound developing moves...
White recaptures.

f6 was played. 

A bit of an overreaction, as Qb6 or Be7 were better.
White gave a check because they saw they could...
Hence c5 at this particular juncture damages White's center for the rest of the game.
Black's just pushing wood at this point.
This is also possible...
Black pushes c3.
And White went to b7, attacking nothing, for some reason.

And White just castled...

White didn't pay attention to what Black was threatening.
Black had to block the check, so that means the knight is safe (for now).
Black pushed...

So White took it.

Now Rh8+ x-rays the rook!

Black correctly just took the knight!

The undefended piece fell!
The king went to d6.
The rook recaptures...
White can win a pawn...
And White now wins the last rook.
Yet White is safe.
Qb6, attacking b2 and d4, was a one move solution to the one move threat of Bg5.

Another check, improving Black's king! 

The rook was good on the 7th rank preventing the Black king from getting out of the 8th rank! 

Now, White's just chasing the king, improving its position!
White defended the pawn.
The rook took on e7...
The king went up.
And now you win the rook.
Black went over to attack the weakened pawns...
And the knight came back to f5 because there was nothing better to do.
This isn't even a threat.

And Black proposed a rook trade. 

But this move is losing.

What can the opponent play after this?

Be7, developing the bishop, blocking the attack, was also better than what was played in the game. 

It completely counters White's idea.
What about trying to end the game right away?
Black saved their a-pawn.
The rook went to d8.
The rook came to c5, threatening nothing...
The rook went to d5. Again, this doesn't threaten anything, but at least it removes the rook from the x-ray threat.
Even if the king gets close it can't defend.
Black traded bishops...
Black recaptured, but now the structure is permanently damaged.
White recaptured.
White placed their knight on a nice outpost.
White got out of the way.

And now g4 was played! 

This move doesn't do anything about f2 hanging or about attacking b7. 

Basically, g4 isn't good for defense nor good for attacking! It's just bad! 

Remember that the aggressive options were b4 and the defensive option was f3.
And now the knight was brought to f5.

Black went to c5, defending c6.

This move also threatens d3 with some counterplay.
The king moved up to a better position.
Black achieved the traditional Caro structure.

And now a meaningless move. 

There was no reason to play this over the resign button. 

Black should have just stalled (refer to the defending chapter!)
Check!
You play the little windmill.
Check!
And now White finally decided to try winning.

White defended with the knight. 

c3 was also possible.
The bishop recaptured.
The king goes back.
Another check.
A normal Caro-Kann.
White correctly captured this pawn.
Black developed the knight.
Black went for the Caro-Kann.

And if you're a beginner, you want to go for the simplest continuation possible. 

With Nxe8 there's no danger.

Now, this move is technically possible. 

This doesn't overlook anything. 

But in chess sometimes it's good to not allow your opponent even the opportunity of counterplay.

For example, what's White's potential counterplay here? 

When there's a pinned piece, the counterplay is: adding more attackers to the pinned piece.

Black must unpin before this can happen!
The knight recaptured.
And now Black wins a piece.
Black moves the king.

And Black went d4, just attacking the knight for one move...

The knight can easily move out of the way, so d4 accomplishes nothing! 

If anything, it weakens the d4 pawn because it is undefended!
Check.
